sections.title: Overview
sections.content: Corrosive agents are substances that cause tissue damage upon contact. Ingestion, inhalation, or dermal exposure to these agents can lead to significant morbidity and mortality. Corrosive agents are chemicals (acids or alkalis) that can cause immediate and progressive damage to tissues on contact. Contamination can occur as a result of accidental occupational exposure or deliberate exposure/ingestion for self-harm.

sections.title: Clinical Presentation
sections.content:
- Ingestion
- Intra oral, oesophageal and/or abdominal pain, burns and/or ulcerations
- Drooling, dysphagia
- Nausea/vomiting
- Risk of perforation and sepsis
- Inhaled
- Dyspnoea, stridor, wheeze, cough, hoarse voice, pulmonary oedema
- Topical Exposure
- Localised pain, burning, blistering, numbness
- Skin discolouration (redness, severe cases may blacken or char skin), rash, cracked, dry or peeling skin, swelling
- Eye exposure
- Burning, stinging, redness, visual disturbance/loss, orbital swelling, lacrimation

sections.title: Considerations
sections.content:
- Responder safety is paramount. Do not approach an unsafe scene
- One or more patients may indicate a hazardous atmosphere. Do not approach until the scene has been declared safe after appropriate monitoring
- Investigating why/how the exposure occurred is important to rule out self-harm
- The absence of lip or oral burns does not exclude significant gastro-oesophageal injury
- The extent of direct chemical injury depends on the pH, concentration, physical form, and duration of contact
- In cases of ingestion, do not induce vomiting and do not attempt neutralisation
- Oxygen should generally be withheld in paraquat toxicity unless the patient is hypoxic; if used, titrate to the minimum required to treat hypoxaemia
- All patients with hydrofluoric acid exposure require hospital transfer after initial treatment

sections.title: Management – Primary
sections.content:
- Primary Survey
- Offer continuous support and reassurance
- Position the patient to protect the airway where needed
- Administer oxygen only if indicated
- Provide assisted ventilation if required
- Remove contaminated clothing and constrictive jewellery
- Consider Diphoterine if available and immediately accessible, but do not delay copious water irrigation
- For dermal or ocular exposure, initiate irrigation immediately and continue during transport where required
- For ingestion, do not induce vomiting and do not attempt neutralisation; monitor closely for airway compromise
- Record vital signs, complete focused secondary survey, and contact Poisons Information or the on-call doctor early

sections.title: Management – Advanced
sections.content:
- Consider advanced airway if evolving upper-airway injury is suspected
- Apply cardiac monitoring and obtain 12 lead ECG where indicated
- Consider IV access
- Consider fluids if indicated
- Consider antiemetic therapy
- Consider IV analgesia in consultation with the on-call doctor
- Consider calcium gluconate gel, solution, or nebuliser depending on the exposure route of hydrofluoric acid
- Escalate early for stridor, dysphagia, chest symptoms, suspected perforation, or significant pain

sections.title: Additional Information
sections.content: - Always offer engagement with Mind Matters or EAP if applicable
